title: Asthma overview
content: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory airway disorder with variable, usually reversible airflow obstruction. Core features include bronchoconstriction, mucus hypersecretion, mucosal edema, airway hyperresponsiveness, and over time airway remodeling with smooth muscle hypertrophy, fibrosis, and basement membrane thickening.

illustrations.caption: Conceptual comparison: narrowed versus relaxed airway (reference illustration).

title: Early-phase and late-phase inflammatory response
content: Early asthma involves allergen-driven IgE cross-linking on mast cells and basophils with release of histamine, prostaglandins, and leukotrienes that rapidly narrow the airway. The late phase recruits eosinophils, T cells, basophils, and neutrophils, sustaining inflammation, edema, mucus production, and airway injury.

illustrations.caption: Conceptual overview: IgE-mediated mast-cell activation (open-license reference figure).

hotspots.label: IgE / FcεRI
hotspots.description: Allergen-specific IgE bound to high-affinity receptors primes mast cells for activation.

hotspots.label: Mast cell
hotspots.description: Cross-linking triggers degranulation and release of histamine, leukotrienes, and other mediators.

hotspots.label: Released mediators
hotspots.description: Vasoactive and inflammatory mediators drive early bronchoconstriction and mucus secretion.

title: Physiology of airflow limitation
content:
Asthma is an obstructive lung disease in which airway narrowing sharply raises resistance and reduces expiratory flow. Pulmonary function testing typically shows reduced FEV1 and reduced FEV1/FVC, with improvement after bronchodilator use. Air trapping increases work of breathing and contributes to hyperinflation during exacerbations.

![Flow-volume loop patterns](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Flow_volume_loops.png/500px-Flow_volume_loops.png)

title: Normal airway versus asthmatic airway histology
content: Normal conducting airways are lined by ciliated pseudostratified columnar epithelium with goblet cells and a relatively thin basement membrane. Asthmatic airways show epithelial shedding, goblet-cell hyperplasia, eosinophilic inflammation, increased vascular permeability, subepithelial fibrosis, smooth muscle hypertrophy, and greater mucus burden.

title: Controller therapy and stepwise management
content: Asthma treatment should be stepped according to symptom burden and physiologic impairment. Inhaled corticosteroids suppress airway inflammation and reduce exacerbations, while rescue bronchodilators treat acute bronchospasm. Before escalating chronic therapy, adherence and inhaler technique should be checked carefully because poor control may reflect medication misuse rather than inadequate treatment intensity.

title: Acute and chronic airway remodeling in Hailey's case
content: During acute exacerbation, eosinophils, mast cells, mucus hypersecretion, epithelial injury, and mucosal edema narrow the airway. Repeated episodes can produce basement membrane thickening, subepithelial fibrosis, smooth muscle hypertrophy, glandular hypertrophy, and persistent Th2-skewed inflammation that make attacks more likely and airflow limitation less reversible over time.
